title: The Two Samaritans And The Tramp
content:
آ آ آ آ A tramp was trampinï؟½ on the road,
آ آ آ آ The afternoon was warm anï؟½ muggy,
آ آ آ آ And by-and-by he chanced to meet
آ آ آ آ A parsin ridinï؟½ in a buggy.
آ آ آ آ Said he: ï؟½As follerers ov the Loard,
آ آ آ آ To do good offices we oughter!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ from a water-bag he poured,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ guv the tramp, a drink er water.

آ آ آ آ The parsin he went rattlinï؟½ ï؟½ome
آ آ آ آ To ware his fam-i-lee was thrivinï؟½,
آ آ آ آ The tramp went on until he met
آ آ آ آ A bullick-driver, bullick drivinï؟½,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Itï؟½s bilinï؟½ ï؟½ot,ï؟½ the driver sed
آ آ آ آ As soonï؟½s the dirty tramp drawed nearer,
آ آ آ آ And from a little keg he poured,
آ آ آ آ And giv the tramp a pint of beer, ï؟½ah!ï؟½

(P.S., The ï؟½ahï؟½ is meant to stand for the tramp a-drinking ov it.)

آ آ آ آ I ainï؟½t agin the temperance cause,
آ آ آ آ Nor yet no advocate ov drinkinï؟½,
آ آ آ آ I only tells the yarn because,
آ آ آ آ Well, at the time it somehow seemed
آ آ آ آ Ter kind ov set me thinkinï؟½.
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
